content.text: SLA Breach Escalation Process

An SLA breach occurs when a support ticket is not responded to or resolved within the committed time window. The breach itself is not the primary problem — breaches will happen in any high-volume support operation. The problem is what happens after the breach: if a breach is not escalated and acknowledged immediately, the ticket continues to age without progress, compounding the delay and the customer impact.

The SLA breach escalation process defines the exact steps, timing, and ownership chain that activates the moment a breach occurs. For GenAI platforms, the escalation process must account for the full range of issue types — from infrastructure outages (clear fix path) to model quality regressions (long investigation path) to safety events (immediate executive visibility regardless of SLA status).

attrs.alt: SLA breach escalation ladder with time thresholds and ownership at each level

content.text: The Escalation Ladder

The escalation ladder defines who becomes involved at each stage past the SLA breach point. Each level adds management visibility and resource pressure without creating noise for the entire organization on every breach.
T+0: Breach Threshold
The moment the SLA clock expires, three things happen automatically:
The ticketing system marks the ticket as  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'SLA breached', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  and sends an alert to the assigned engineer and their direct manager
The breach is logged in the SLA breach register — a persistent record of all breaches with timestamps, ticket IDs, customer tiers, and root causes that feeds the monthly SLA report
A customer-facing status update is prepared (but not sent yet) — the assigned engineer has 15 minutes to acknowledge and review the update before it is sent

The 15-minute grace window before the status update is sent gives the engineer a chance to provide an accurate update rather than an automated holding message. If the engineer does not acknowledge within 15 minutes, the next escalation level activates.
T+15 Minutes: Support Manager Engagement
If the assigned engineer has not acknowledged the breach alert within 15 minutes:
The support manager is paged directly (not emailed — a page requires immediate response)
The support manager assumes ownership of the ticket and either reassigns it to an available engineer or begins working it directly
The account manager is notified for enterprise-tier customers — they need to know an SLA breach is occurring so they can proactively manage the customer relationship

A customer status update is sent: 'We are aware that your ticket has not received a response within our committed window. A senior member of our team is now personally engaged and you will receive an update within [30 minutes].'

The 15-minute threshold assumes the engineer is working but did not notice the alert. If a breach occurs during off-hours for business-hours SLA tiers, the timer should start when the next business day begins — the escalation clock runs on SLA business hours, not wall clock time.
T+1 Hour: Engineering Manager Engagement
If no meaningful progress has been made 1 hour after the breach:
The engineering manager is engaged — not for awareness, but for active intervention
A second engineer is assigned to assist with investigation (for P1 issues)
For enterprise customers: the engineering manager calls or messages the account manager to align on the customer communication strategy
If the issue involves a model quality regression: the ML engineering lead is notified and asked to prioritize
The 1-hour mark is the point at which 'the engineer is working on it' transitions to 'we have a support process failure that needs active management.'
T+4 Hours: Executive Escalation
A breach that has not been resolved or meaningfully progressed after 4 hours is a significant support failure requiring executive visibility:
VP Engineering and VP Customer Success (or equivalent) are briefed
For enterprise customers: an executive from the company (VP or C-level) reaches out directly to the customer's executive sponsor — this is relationship management, not technical support
If the issue is a P1 (complete outage or safety event): a war room is convened with all relevant technical and customer-facing stakeholders
The breach is now treated as a major incident regardless of whether it has been formally declared as one
Safety Event Override
Safety events bypass the entire escalation ladder. The moment a safety event is confirmed, the response goes directly to the highest level without waiting for T+15, T+1h, or T+4h thresholds:
AI Safety team is paged immediately (within minutes, not hours)
Legal is notified if there is any possibility of data exposure or regulatory obligation
VP Engineering is briefed within 15 minutes of confirmation
The standard breach escalation process is suspended for safety events — a different, faster process governs
Breach Root Cause Classification
Every SLA breach must be classified by root cause before it is closed. Root cause classification drives process improvement and identifies systemic issues vs. one-off events.
Root cause category
Definition
Typical corrective action
Staffing gap
Not enough engineers available to handle ticket volume; engineer was overloaded or on leave
Capacity review; on-call coverage audit
Missing evidence
SLA clock ran while waiting for customer to provide required information; clock was not paused correctly
Review clock-pause rules; ensure 'needs-info' status pauses the SLA clock
Complexity underestimated
Initial priority assessment was too low; P2 assigned to what was effectively P1
Review triage criteria; re-train classifying engineers
Dependency on external team
Fix required ML team or platform team input; they did not respond in time
Establish SLA commitments between support and internal teams; escalate earlier
Process gap
No clear escalation path for this type of issue; engineer did not know who to contact
Document escalation path; update playbook
System failure
Ticketing system or alerting did not fire the breach alert correctly
Audit alert configuration; test breach detection in staging

Root causes are logged per-breach and reviewed monthly. Three or more breaches with the same root cause in a month constitute a systemic issue requiring a formal corrective action plan, not just individual acknowledgement.
Customer Communication at Breach

How a company communicates during an SLA breach often matters more to the customer than the breach itself. A breach handled with transparency and urgency can preserve — or even strengthen — the customer relationship. A breach ignored or minimized destroys trust.
Communication Principles During a Breach

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Acknowledge immediately', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — don't wait until you have something to say. 'We are aware your ticket has not received a response within our committed window' is better than silence.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Be specific, not vague', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — 'We expect to have an update within 30 minutes' is better than 'We are working on it.' Specific commitments can be missed; vague ones create indefinite uncertainty.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Own the breach', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — don't blame the customer's report being complex or the issue being hard to reproduce. The SLA is a commitment the company made; when it is missed, the company owns the miss.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Update at every commitment', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — if you said 'update in 30 minutes,' send one in 30 minutes whether or not you have a resolution. An update that says 'still investigating, next update in 30 minutes' is better than missing the commitment again.
Communication Templates by Tier

BREACH COMMUNICATION: Enterprise P1 (T+0 — initial acknowledgement)
---------------------------------------------------------------------
From: [Account Manager name]
To: [Customer executive contact]
Subject: Response delay on your Priority 1 ticket [ID]

[Customer name],

I want to let you know personally that your Priority 1 ticket [ID] has
not received a response within our committed 30-minute window. This is
not acceptable and I am personally engaged to ensure it receives
immediate attention.

Our engineering team is now actively working on your case. You will receive
a technical update within the next 30 minutes.

I will also follow up with you directly. Please reach me at [direct number]
if you need to speak immediately.

[Account Manager name]

---

BREACH COMMUNICATION: Enterprise P2 (T+15 min — after manager pickup)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
From: Support team
To: [Customer contact]
Subject: Update on your support request [ID]

Thank you for your patience. We want to acknowledge that your request [ID]
did not receive our first response within the expected timeframe.

A senior member of our technical team is now actively reviewing your case.
You can expect a substantive update within [30 minutes / 2 hours depending
on investigation complexity].

We apologize for the delay. Ticket reference: [ID]

---

BREACH COMMUNICATION: Mid-investigation update (recurring every N minutes)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------
From: [Engineer name], Support Engineering
To: [Customer contact]
Subject: Investigation update — Ticket [ID]

Investigation status: [In progress / Awaiting information from X team]

What we know: [Specific technical finding from investigation so far]
What we are doing next: [Next action with responsible engineer name]
Next update: [Specific time — not 'soon']

If you need to speak with someone urgently: [Manager direct contact]
Post-Breach Review Process

Every P1 breach and every pattern of P2 breaches requires a post-breach review. This is distinct from the incident post-incident review (PIR) — a breach review focuses on the support process failure, not the underlying technical issue.
Individual Breach Review (P1 breaches)
For every P1 SLA breach, conduct a brief review within 48 hours of the breach being closed:
What was the ticket? (brief description, customer tier, priority)
What caused the breach? (root cause from the classification above)
What happened at each escalation level? (did the escalation process work as designed?)
What should change to prevent this root cause from causing a breach again?
Are service credits owed? (apply the formula from the SLA contract)
The review is documented in the breach register, not discussed verbally. Documentation is what allows patterns to be identified across many breaches over time.
Monthly Breach Pattern Review
Monthly, the support manager reviews all breaches from the prior month:
Total breaches by tier and priority
Root cause distribution
Repeat root causes (same cause appearing 3+ times = systemic issue)
Service credits paid and total financial impact
Any breach that triggered executive escalation

Systemic issues identified in the monthly review are assigned a corrective action owner with a deadline. They are tracked in the following month's review to confirm the corrective action was implemented.
Service Credit Calculation and Payment

Service credits are the financial remedy for SLA breaches. The credit calculation must be precise, timely, and proactively applied — do not make enterprise customers discover breaches and ask for credits. Credits are calculated from the breach register and communicated in the monthly SLA report.
Credit Calculation Workflow

# Monthly credit calculation (run at end of each calendar month)

def calculate_monthly_credits(customer_id, month):
    contract = get_contract(customer_id)
    breaches = get_breaches(customer_id, month)
    monthly_fee = contract.monthly_fee

    credits = []

    # Uptime credits
    downtime_minutes = calculate_downtime(customer_id, month)
    guaranteed_uptime = contract.uptime_sla  # e.g., 0.999
    allowed_downtime = (1 - guaranteed_uptime) * 30 * 24 * 60  # minutes per month
    excess_downtime = max(0, downtime_minutes - allowed_downtime)

    if excess_downtime > 0:
        uptime_credit = calculate_uptime_credit(excess_downtime, monthly_fee)
        credits.append({"type": "uptime", "amount": uptime_credit,
                        "detail": f"{excess_downtime:.1f} min excess downtime"})

    # Support response SLA credits
    p1_breaches = [b for b in breaches if b.priority == "P1"]
    p2_breaches = [b for b in breaches if b.priority == "P2"]

    # Apply grace breach rule (first P1/P2 breach per month = no credit)
    if len(p1_breaches) > 1:
        for breach in p1_breaches[1:]:  # Skip first (grace)
            credits.append({
                "type": "support_response_P1",
                "amount": monthly_fee * 0.05,  # 5% per breach
                "detail": f"P1 breach: Ticket {breach.ticket_id}"
            })

    total_credit = min(sum(c["amount"] for c in credits),
                       monthly_fee * contract.max_credit_pct)

    return {
        "customer_id": customer_id,
        "month": month,
        "credits": credits,
        "total_credit": total_credit,
        "applied_to": "next_invoice"
    }

Credits are applied proactively to the next invoice — the customer does not need to request them. Proactive credit application demonstrates accountability and reduces the friction of the breach experience.
Breach Prevention Through Leading Indicators

Breach escalation is a reactive process. A mature support operation also runs a proactive process that identifies tickets at risk of breaching before the deadline passes, allowing intervention without triggering the escalation ladder.
The SLA Risk Queue

Maintain a separate view in the ticketing system showing all tickets that have consumed more than 60% of their SLA window without resolution. This queue is reviewed twice daily by the support manager:
Any ticket at > 60% SLA time remaining with no recent update gets a proactive owner check
Any ticket blocked on a customer response is confirmed to have the SLA clock paused
Any ticket pending L3 response is checked to ensure L3 is actively engaged

Intervening at 60% prevents most breaches before they occur. The cost is 10 minutes of manager attention twice daily — far lower than the customer communication and credit overhead of an actual breach.
SLA Risk Signals Specific to GenAI Issues
GenAI quality issues have predictable patterns that signal high breach risk:
Risk signal
Why it predicts breach risk
Proactive action
Quality complaint with no reproduction after 2h of L2 investigation
Non-reproducible issues can run indefinitely without progress
L3 review; decide whether to escalate or close as 'cannot reproduce'
Issue classified as model regression but ML team not yet engaged
ML team dependency creates a wait time outside support's control
Escalate to ML lead proactively; do not wait for the SLA clock to run out
Enterprise customer, issue age > 50% of SLA, no update in 1h
Enterprise breaches trigger executive escalation; prevent with early update
Send a proactive status update before SLA breach; reset customer expectation
Safety-flagged ticket with 'investigating' status for > 30 min
Safety issues should be acknowledged by Safety team within 30 min; any longer suggests the fast path did not work
Confirm Safety team received the escalation; page if not confirmed
Capacity-Driven Breach Prevention

The highest-risk period for SLA breaches is when ticket volume spikes unexpectedly — a model update, a viral social media prompt pattern, or a major customer onboarding all drive sudden volume increases that can overwhelm normal staffing.
Maintain a breach risk escalation threshold: if the open P1/P2 ticket count exceeds 150% of the team's normal capacity, the support manager triggers the surge response:
Alert engineering management that support is at surge capacity
Request temporary additional L2/L3 capacity from the engineering team
For enterprise customers: proactively notify account managers that response times may be extended and provide a specific ETA for normalization
Post a status page banner if the volume spike is related to a platform-level issue
Summary

The SLA breach escalation process converts a missed deadline into a managed response. The escalation ladder activates at T+0 (breach alert and customer acknowledgement), T+15 minutes (support manager if no engineer acknowledgement), T+1 hour (engineering manager and second resource), and T+4 hours (executive escalation and war room for P1). Safety events bypass the ladder entirely and go directly to the highest level of response. Every breach is classified by root cause; three or more breaches with the same root cause in a month trigger a formal corrective action. Customer communication during a breach follows specific principles — acknowledge immediately, be specific, own the miss, update at every promised interval. Service credits are calculated proactively from the breach register and applied to the next invoice without requiring the customer to request them.
Key takeaways:
Four-level escalation ladder: T+0 (owner alert), T+15min (manager if no ack), T+1h (eng manager + second resource), T+4h (executive)
Safety events bypass the ladder — immediate Safety team + Legal + VP Engineering notification at any time
Every breach has a logged root cause; three same-cause breaches in a month = systemic issue requiring corrective action
Customer communication: acknowledge immediately, be specific, own the miss, commit to and meet update intervals
Service credits are calculated proactively and applied to the next invoice — customers should not have to ask
The first breach per customer per month is a grace breach for response SLA; subsequent breaches trigger credits
